data.source: Laboratory experiments using decision tasks administered to university student participants
data.notes: Participants made incentivised binary choices; one choice per participant was randomly selected for payment with immediate or post-dated checks.

method.description: Two controlled experiments primed participants’ social identities (ethnicity, race, or gender) through background questionnaires and then measured changes in time discounting and risk aversion using standard binary-choice tasks. Reservation interest rates and risk premiums were estimated and analysed with interval regressions.
steps: Recruit undergraduate participants from U.S. universities (Temple University and University of Michigan for Experiment 2; unnamed university for Experiment 1).
Randomly assign participants to identity-salience or control conditions using background questionnaires that include or omit identity-related questions.
Elicit time preferences with 46 binary choices between smaller-sooner and larger-later monetary payments.
Elicit risk preferences with 18 binary choices between a certain amount and a gamble.
Randomly select one decision for real payment via immediate or post-dated checks.
Compute reservation interest rates and risk premiums for each participant.
Estimate treatment effects with interval regression models on the log of the reservation interest rate and the risk premium, including control dummies for time horizon, stake size, and institution.
method.models: Interval regression on log reservation interest rate and log risk premium
method.outcome_variable: Reservation interest rate (discount rate) and reservation risk premium
method.independent_variables: Identity-salience treatment dummy, demographic group indicators (Asian, White, Native Black, Immigrant Black, gender), interaction terms
method.control_variables: Time horizon dummy (1 week vs. 2 weeks), stakes dummy ($100 sure payout), institution dummy (University of Michigan), constant term

results.summary: Making social identities salient reduced impatience among Asian-American and native Black participants and, for native Blacks who believed the payment promises, increased risk aversion. No significant effects were found for Whites, immigrant Blacks, or gender salience on either impatience or risk attitudes.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Median reservation interest rate – Asian-American subjects
Log reservation interest rate coefficient – Native Blacks (Michigan sample)
Risk premium coefficient – Native Black believers (Michigan sample)
